sentences.text: Yes.
I came back and next day, I had to go out and work again.
And what was I going to do again?
I was going to dig ditches again.
And this time they said to my mother, you have to go.
And my sister, you have to go.
So I found one of the-- not SS, because he was not a-- one-- one of the German guards.
I went over to him.
I says, please.
I begged him to leave my mother there, because she didn't have any strength, and-- and leave my sister there because she didn't have any strength, to leave them in a-- in a camp, which he did.
He granted it.
He says, all right.
That's fine with me.
Well, anyway, we went in.
I said to my mother, you know something.
I says, I'll give you one more day.
I says, you know, we're going to be liberated soon.
I says, we're going be coming-- we-- we're going to be going back to the same way we came.
We're going to take the same path as we came here.
Were going to go back home.
And actually, my sister was a pessimist.
And she was very sick.
And she didn't believe me.
Anyway, next day I went out, and I dug the trenches.
And by that time, I guess my-- my-- my muscles were giving out a little bit too.
And I didn't finish the third part that I was supposed to.
And they came over to me and with a bayonet.
And they were going to hit me.
And this soldier that I told you befriended me happened to see it.
And he start to talk to them.
And he talked them out of hitting me.
And they let me in.
Next morning, that was that day.
I came back and naturally, I found my mother and my sister in the same condition as-- as much as-- as I left them, very, very poor.
My sister didn't stop crying.
She was-- she says, I'm going to die here.
I'm going to die here, because they weren't-- they didn't really pay any attention to her.
They didn't give her any food.
I mean, she was really starving.
And I kept on telling her, you're going to see.
We're going to be liberated.
I-- I-- I can hear from a distance.
I hear something that is-- I bet you, that's the bombs.
I said they're bombing some place around here.
She says, no, no.
I said they're bombing, because I can hear it.
I can hear it.
I found a girl, a Polish girl, who befriended-- who was befriended by a German soldier.
And I said to her, did you hear anything, any news at all?
She says, not really.
But I can tell you something.
Says they're bombing all over the place here.
She says, I bet you anything, she says, I heard the radio, because he let me listen to a radio.
We will be out of here in about two days.
So I went back.
And I said to my mother, and I swore.
I said please, believe me, because I heard it, my own ears.
I say, I heard it.
We're going to be liberated.
Next morning, [INAUDIBLE] it was like about 4 o'clock in the morning.
The barrack where we were, we had windows.
But, of course, they had the bars on-- on all the windows.
I looked out the window, and I saw a very familiar face that was one of the SS men.
They were there in-- in civilian clothes.
And I said to my mother-- I woke her up.
I says, mom, I say, you wouldn't believe it.
I says, he's in civilian clothes.
She says, I cannot believe this.
I-- probably, he's going on a-- for a trip.
I says, he's in civilian clothes.
And it took me another like 15 minutes.
I saw a lot of them in-- a lot of soldiers in civilian clothes.
I said this is it.
This is the day that we're going to be liberated.
And I always told my sister, we'll be liberated before my birthday, which is May 6th.
That morning, early morning, they locked all the gates.
They locked all our doors.
We weren't able to get out of our barracks.
They just locked us in.
They didn't say a word.
And they disappeared.
And all of a sudden, in a distance, I heard Russian singing.
The Russian soldiers were singing as they were coming closer to the camp.
I don't think they knew what they're going to find.
And they broke down the gate.
And they came, and they opened our door.
And they said, you are free to go anywhere you want.
Now, where were we going to go?
You know, you're so stunned.
So what are you going to do?
What can you do?
The first thing I did, there was a building where they were keeping all the food.
I said to my mother, I'm not going to bring you fatty food.
I don't know why.
I'm-- I don't know why I said it.
I said, I'm going to bring you something.
I'm going to go and steal.
And-- and people were just jamming that place.
I had-- I had to crawl between people's legs to get a little bit closer to the food.
I took two pieces of bread, two-- two loaves of bread and margarine that was in, like, in a block, in a square.
I took one square of that.
And I put it under my-- my jacket.
And I held it close to my body.
And as I came out of the building, a Russian woman, who was also a-- a-- a prisoner there got a hold of me.
And she beat me down to the ground, and she stole my bread.
And I says, I don't believe it.
But I was lucky because one bread I had like in a back of my-- my-- back.
One-- one I had in a front, and one I had in the back, because they were big breads.
So I put one in the back, one in a front.
And the one she stole was in the front.
I thought myself, that's OK.
I still have one bread.
I still have something to eat.
And I came back to the barrack.
And my sister says, is it really happening?
I said, it happened.
I say, you are free.
You want to walk out, you can walk out.
She wouldn't get out of the-- of the building.
I took her by her hand.
And I says, come with me.
I says, come with me.
I want you to see.
This is freedom.
I said, from now on, you can do anything you want.
You can go anywhere you want.
She says, but I'm so sick, I'm going to die.
I says, not anymore.
I said, you're not going to die because we're going to help you.
And that was our liberation from the camp.
It was the last day being a prisoner in Neustadt-Glewe, which was quite an experience.
Very nice.
They were very kind, very generous.
They gave us their food, whatever they had on their tanks.
They came with-- with cars.
They came with trucks.
And whatever food they had on a truck, they dispersed it between-- between us.
And they were very generous.
It was very nice.
It was a beautiful day.
I didn't know what I was going to do next day, but it was a beautiful day.
I never-- never forget it as long as I live.
To be free again, it was the most precious thing in the world.
And I said to my mother, there is God.
God is there.
He's the one who did it.
We are still here.
We are still alive.
We are still walking.
We are still upright.
It was a beautiful day for me and for all of us.
The next day, [SIGHS] we went where the Germans used to have their barracks.
We took a room that was available and because they had a mattress.
So I said to my mom, this is luxury.
And you're going to sleep in bed.
My sister, her bones were just-- she was just skin and bone.
I said, you and mom are going to sleep on this bed.
And I says, I don't have to.
I says, because I can find another place to sleep.
I say, you're going to be sleeping on a bed.
So I put them in this room.
And with any strength I had, I got together with the-- this other girl that I-- I knew from before, and we start to walk toward town.
I had no idea where we were going.
We were just walking.
And I says, wherever we'll go, I'll go in and I'll ask them for some food, whoever's going to be there.
So we walked into the nearest town.
And I cannot remember the name of the town, the nearest.
I probably just-- it must have been Neustadt-Glewe itself, the-- the city itself.
As we came to the outskirts of town, there was a-- a building.
And I walked in.
And it was like a grocery store.
And I walked in, and there was rice.
I says, oh my god, how wonderful.
This is rice.
As I was reaching to get some rice in a piece of paper that I found on a floor, I saw something shining.
And I said to my friend, something is wrong.
As I-- as I looked closer, they put pieces of glass in between the rice.
If they-- so if they couldn't have it, we couldn't have it.
From then, I went, and we came over to a-- a place, and I heard chicken.
I says, oh my god.
I said, there's chicken.
Someplace there's chicken.
I said there is a barn.
Let's go down to the barn.
Well, We walked toward the barn, and a German woman and a man came and says, you can't go anywhere.
You cannot go any further than this.
You had better turn around and get out of here.
I said to them, look.
I was in the camp.
I'm hungry.
And I would like to have something to eat.
I said, do you have any food?
No, we don't have any food at all for you.
And they chased us out.
So I went out on the street.
And as it happens, a German-- a Russian soldier was at the door-- at the door-- by the fence.
And I said to him in Russian, I said, they wouldn't give us any food.
He says, come on.
I'll help you.
So he came in with us.
And he said to the German, don't you dare to be in their way.
They can take anything they want.
Well, I went.
And I found a chicken.
And I tied the-- the feet.
And I went in a house.
And I got hold of-- all I wanted was clean underwear and-- and clean shoes, which I couldn't get because nothing fit me, and just clean clothes, period, and some food.
I went in and I got hold of a sheet from a bed.
I pulled the sheet off the bed.
I didn't know where to look for, because I didn't have the patience to go look for it.
I took a sheet off the bed.
And I made a-- for my back, a-- a backpack.
And I put the chicken in the backpack.
And I went in the kitchen.
And I asked her, do you have any food here?
She says, yes.
And there was a bread.
I took the bread.
And I said, do you have anything?
There was margarine.
I took some margarine.
And-- and then I went down in the garden, and I needed some vegetables.
I says, if my mother is going to make me chicken soup, I'd better have some vegetables with it.
I went and I got some vegetables.
And that's all I wanted.
I didn't want to have jewelry.
I saw people stopping people on a-- Germans on the street and-- and stripping them of their-- of their jewelry, of their rings and everything.
I had no desire to do that.
I just wanted to be clean.
And I just wanted to eat something.
So as I was walking home, all of a sudden the backpack felt very, very light.
And I said to my friend, I says, I don't know.
I said, why is it feeling so light?
I lost the chicken.
A chicken flew out of my-- my backpack.
That made me turn around, and I went back.
And I started all over again.
And I-- and I got again a chicken.
And I went back.
And one of the men that was also a prisoner, you know, of war, and he was not a Jewish man.
And I said to him, look, I cannot kill a chicken.
I says, and we need this chicken to make chicken soup.
Could you please help me?
And he went and he helped me.
He-- he cut the chicken open for me.
And he cleaned it for me.
And that was our very first day being liberated.
And it was the most beautiful thing that we lived through, that one-- one day.
Of course, we didn't eat a lot because I wouldn't dare to give my sister a lot of food, because she was so sick, and my mother neither, you know, just a little bit, a little.
I told her a little at a time.
I don't know where I got it from at that time.
I had no knowledge.
I didn't know that that's all you can eat is a little at the time.
But I felt, you know, they hadn't eaten-- we hadn't eaten so long, you know?
Rich food, we better not eat a lot of food because I saw people bending over.
And they were very, very sick around me.
So I thought it's from the food, probably.
So that was my first day.
How long did you stay in Neustadt-Glewe?
In Neustadt-Glewe, we only stayed like three days, because we were so anxious to get out of there and-- and start the-- the journey back home, which we didn't know which way to go.
We had no idea where we were.
I mean, you know, we found out that we were next to Hamburg.
Well, I wasn't going to go to Hamburg, because going back to Czechoslovakia is the opposite way.
So we start.
We asked somebody which way is south to go down south.
And he told us.
And then we start to walk.
And as we were walking, we encountered-- [SIGHS] we were like 10 of us, 10 girls.
And all of a sudden in the distance we saw Russian soldiers, Cossacks.
And my mother had a horrible, horrible story to tell us about Cossacks, how, you know, they rape women and-- and they're very mean.
So I was very apprehensive of even talking to them.
They came close, and one stopped.
The commander of-- of that unit stopped and started to talk to me.
And I told him, I said, [NON-ENGLISH], which is means Jewish, Jewish.
And he just didn't understand what it is.
Anyway, finally, he realized that we were Jewish people.
And I told him-- I showed him my-- my number on my arm.
And he didn't know what the number was because he never saw it before.
And I couldn't explain it to him because I spoke a Russian, but not such a wonderful Russian that I was able to explain everything to him.
He went, and he took everybody's drink-- what do you-- they have it in their-- I don't know-- what they carry.
Their canteen, whatever it was.
And they took away from everybody a canteen of water and gave us the water.
And as I found out later on, after we were all through and I says, we are tired.
We want to go in the city.
He says, I'm going to go with you.
And he actually walked with us to the nearest little village.
And he went in.
And he said to the-- the people in this village, in this building, he says, I want you to make room for these people because they're very tired.
And I want you to be able to give them some food.
And that was my first encounter with Russians, which I don't trust them very much anyway.
But he was nice.
And that was the second day of being free.
So people were doing good things for us.
And that was the beginning of having faith in humanity again after-- after Neustadt.
Third day, we wanted to get back to Prague as fast as we can.
We start to walk again in which we were walking south.
And-- and we came upon a small city.
And we saw a Red Cross.
And I says, oh, there's a Red Cross someplace.
So we found the Red Cross kitchen.
And we walked in.
And they hadn't seen too many people at that time from camps yet.
They had a lot of people in that city, POWs from-- from Denmark and from Denmark and-- and Holland, from both places.
And they put us-- they said we have a place for you to stay, which was a school that they had converted into like a little hotel, a little place to sleep.
And we went into that place.
And we were there not more than a couple hours.
Some people were coming, men were coming in.
And they said, are you one family?
And I said yes, my mother and my sister and myself.
They took us under their wings.
They were POWs.
They were prisoners of war.
And one was a doctor.
One was a dentist.
And-- one-- and-- and one was a policeman.
And they fed-- they-- they told us they are going to feed us.
They're going to find us new clothes.
And they're going to-- we can take showers in their place.
And we don't have to worry about getting food anywhere else.
We don't have to go out to the Red Cross kitchen anymore.
And this went on for about a week until I found out that there is one way, some way for us to get back to Prague.
Also, they showed us the place where I can register my-- register for getting back into Czechoslovakia, which we did.
We went down and we registered to go back to Czechoslovakia.
In two days, there was a truck, an open truck coming in.
And they said, anybody that wants to go back to Czechoslovakia, please be here at 8 o'clock in the morning, which naturally, that was the first thing we did.
We didn't sleep all night because we were so excited.
We went-- we went there.
And they put us on this open truck.
And we drove back to Prague.
It took us a while because we made a few stops.
And we didn't have to worry about food because wherever we went, they gave us free food.
They didn't even question it.
They knew where we were coming from.
So they were-- they were very generous, and they treated us very, very nicely.
And that was in Germany yet.
When we got back to Prague, there was another we didn't know where to go.
So again, we seeked out a-- a Red Cross kitchen to see whether we can get some food, which we did.
They showed us where it was.
And they-- they told us there is another school that was converted into, you know, for sleeping, a little hotel.
And that's where we-- you know, they had cots, you know, one after-- you know, open, naturally, open space with cots.
And that's where we settled down.
But my sister and my mother were not feeling well.
So I said to one of the ladies that was in charge, I says, you know, my sister's not feeling good and my mother doesn't either.
So she told us to go to the nearest hospital, which was not too far.
We went to the near-- it wasn't-- it was not-- yeah, it was a-- set up for a hospital.
We went to the nearest place, and they checked my sister and checked my mother.
And they said, yes, they have to be hospitalized because they were really very, very sick.
And they put them in a hospital.
And I stayed behind in this school.
And I lived there.
I visited them, naturally, every day, practically every-- you know.
I was there all day with them.
And then my sister one day said to me, I'm going to die if-- if I don't go home.
I have to go back home to my hometown.
I says, I really don't want to.
She says, well, I have to, because I know I'm going to die.

So my mother says, if she feels that way, she says, why don't you try to get her out of the hospital and see if you can take a train and go down, because it was quite-- quite a ways from Prague to-- to Eastern Czechoslovakia where we were.
So I picked her up one day.
And they let her go for two days or three days.
And we took the train and went.
We went back to my hometown.
And when we arrived in-- in my hometown, I went to my house.
And we couldn't go through the gate, because they wouldn't let us.
That was the Russian converted our house into secret police headquarters.
And they would not let us go through.
So I went to our neighbor that we were friends with from before.
And my sister wanted to go back.
That was the only reason that she wanted to go back, because she wanted to prove it to everybody in our town that we are still alive.
So we went to our neighbor.
And they gave us a space to sleep.
And we stayed with them a few days.
And it was lice-infected.
So were we before, because in-- in-- in the camp, we were infected with lice, naturally.
And we stayed there for about two days.
And I said to-- I went next door to my house.
And I said to them, please let me in.
I said, you know, we have an orchard down there.
And I says, all the fruit is just beginning to ripen.
I just would like to look around.
Let me look around.
He says, no.
It's not yours anymore.
I wouldn't let you in.
And then next day, there was another soldier is guarding the gate.
And I told him what I want to do.
He says, don't let anybody said I'll let you in.
So he left me in.
And we went in.
And we had a cellar.
I don't know if I told you.
I didn't.
Before the-- before we left Uzhorod, my father took me down into the cellar.
And it was all brick walled.
And he says, you the youngest in our family.
And if anything happens to us, you-- probably will be you.
You'll survive whatever will happen, which he didn't know ahead of time, naturally.
He took out-- we had a lot of jewelry.
And he took out some jewelry.
And he took out a few-- few bricks from the wall.
And he showed me where.
He says, I'm going to put this one here and this one.
He showed me where he puts the jewelry.
And he put the brick back like, you know, there was no sign at all that he ever touched it.
So I said to my sister, I have to go down a cellar.
I just have to go down there.
I'm going to-- he says-- she says, no, they're going to kill you.
These Russians are very tough.
I says, yeah, well, let me try.
So while he was guarding the gate, I snuck down into the cellar.
And what I saw, it was a very unsightly thing.
Every single brick was out of the wall.
The floor was dug up inch by inch by inch.
And somebody was there who suspected it or who knew what was happening.
Naturally, they took everything away.
So I ended up with nothing, which is-- which was fine with me.
It really was.
My-- my life was spared, so it didn't matter that much.
Next day, we were supposed to go home.
And I had to go and get a permit from the-- from the Russian government.
So they were only giving out one permit.
Now, my sister was too sick and she couldn't go.
So I didn't know what to do.
So I got one permit and I gave it to my sister.
And I says, you go.
You get on a-- on a-- you-- you get on a truck or on a bus.
You go.
And I'll-- somehow, I'll get out of here.
So when we went down to the train station, she got on the-- she got on the bus.
I went on a bus with her.
I said to the guy, I just want to say goodbye to my sister.
I just want to go as far as the train station.
I says, I'm staying behind.
I don't want to go anywhere.
He says fine.
So it was a-- a Russian soldier.
He says, OK.
So he let me go down to the train station.
And my sister got on the-- on a train.
And I walked around the train like I'm pretending I was saying goodbye to her, because everybody had to have a permit to-- to get on a train.
And when he wasn't looking, he turned his back, I snuck up on the train.
[LAUGHS] And I sat down real quick.
And I didn't sit down and nowhere near my sister.
And I just sat down.
And they knew how many permits they had in their hands.
There was one permit short, because he-- he was-- head-- counting heads.
And he came over to me-- not came over to me.
He says, somebody doesn't belong here.
Who is it?
Somebody-- you better tell me, or you're going to be in trouble.
Somebody-- and I pretended I didn't even hear him.
I just played dead.
I played dumb.
And that's how I got out of-- back, out of-- [LAUGHS] from my hometown.
Otherwise, I could not have gotten out of there.
So that's how I got back.
And we went back to-- to Prague.
And I put my sister back in-- in-- in a hospital.
And next day, I went to-- I went back to Slovakia.
I went to Budapest to look for my brother.
I was-- I had a funny feeling.
I had to look for my brother.
I knew my father I don't have to look for, because I found his clothes in-- in the camp when I was sorting the clothes.
I found my father's suit.
So I knew he-- where he was.
I knew he was dead.
When I went-- anyway, [LAUGHS] I forgot my train of thought.
When I went, I said to my-- my mother, I have to go and look for Andy.
Andy should be alive, should be.
She says, well, we haven't heard [INAUDIBLE].
And I was really afraid.
I was afraid to-- to find out what I would find out, that he's not alive.

I went to Budapest to look for him, also on a Red Cross kitchen, because in a Red Cross kitchen, everybody was leaving little notes on the wall, leaving names and-- and-- not telephone numbers, because nobody had a telephone, addresses and where they can be reached or where can they be found.
And from there, I didn't find him.
I went to Slovakia, to Bratislava.
And I was also-- that time, my sister, my mother decided to meet me.
So they met me in Bratislava.
And we're at the Red Cross kitchen.
And I-- and I was writing a note on-- on the wall to see whether, you know, somebody might find my name to let them know that we are-- that I'm alive, and we are alive.
And as I was standing in line, there was a-- a boy looking at me.
And he came over to me, he says, are you Andy's sister?
I says, yes, I am.
I'm looking for Andy.
He says to me, well, don't look any further, because Andy is alive.
OK.
Can we pause right here, then?
OK.
This is the end of tape number four.
